content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Roger D. Wolfertz
Legal Division
Department of Education
721 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA 95814

Date : March 26, 1982

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Diane Maura Fitzgerald, Legal Division

Subject: Council for Private Postsecondary Educational Institutions
Advice File No. A-82-051

I have reviewed the powers and duties of the Council and the
Protocol used by the prior Council. Based on this review and our
telephone discussions, my understanding of the situation is as
follows.

The Council is an advisory body to the Superintendent of
Public Instruction regarding the administration of the Private
Postsecondary Education Act of 1977. It is composed of nonsalaried
members who are appointed by various public officials. It advises
on policy and procedure, but it does not have the power to adopt
regulations or make any other final decisions on the Department
of Education's activities. Education Code Section 94305(f).

However, the Council does participate in proceedings against
educational institutions. The Department is required to "consult"
with the Council on such proceedings. Education Code Section
94305. The statutes do not prescribe how the Department should
consult the Council. Under the current Protocol, prior to taking
any formal action, the Department presents its case in such a
proceeding to the Council; the affected institution can present
its case at the same time. The Council then advises the Department
as to whether, in the Council's opinion, there is probable cause
to pursue the matter. The Department, however, actually makes
the decision on whether there is probable cause to proceed. If
the Department concludes that there is probable cause, the case
proceeds through the normal administrative hearing process, and
the Superintendent makes the final decision on the matter.

You asked whether the members of the Council should be included
in a conflict of interest code pursuant to the financial reporting
provisions of the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections
87300, et seq.¹

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Roger D. Wolfartz
March 26, 1982
Page Two


Section 87302 provides that public officials who participate
in or make decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect
on any financial interest should be governed by a conflict of
interest code. See also Section 3.019. Commission, Regulation,
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700, defines "public official" to
include unsalaried members of boards which make substantive recommendations, which are, and over an extended period of time, have
been, regularly approved by another public official or governmental
agency. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 also defines participation
in a governmental decision to include making substantive recommendations to the decision-maker. Applying these definitions, most of
the Council's actions would not constitute governmental decision-
making. But if the Council makes substantive recommendations in
the probable cause proceedings, and those recommendations are
regularly accepted by the Department, it participates in governmental decision-making as to those proceedings within the meaning
of the Act. A decision finding probable cause to proceed with a
license suspension or revocation proceeding has a material effect
on the affected institution. Sections 7100 - 8703.

However, due to the infrequency of the probable cause proceedings
(7 to 9 in the last two years), the fact that Council membership
has changed and that it is not clear that Council recommendations
as to probable cause will ordinarily be accepted by the Department,
it is not foreseeable that the Council members will make decisions
which will affect financial interests. Therefore, we conclude
that the Council members need not be included in a conflict of
interest code.

As we discussed, however, this conclusion does not mean that
the Act does not apply to the Council members. Section 87100,
which contains the basic prohibition against conflicts of interest,
applies to all public officials, regardless of whether they are
included in a conflict of interest code. Accordingly, if a Council
member has a financial interest in an institution which is before
the Council as a part of the public cause proceeding, and if it
is foreseeable the Council's decision will affect the ultimate
decision as to probable cause, then he or she is required to
disclose the financial interest on the record and disqualify him-
or herself from participating in the proceeding.

Although it is not necessary or required, I would strongly
recommend that Section 8100 or the regulation you suggested be
included in the Council's protocol. It would serve to remind
members of their obligations under the Act.

Please feel free to contact me if you would like to discuss
this further.

DMF;ph

State of California                                    Department of Education

Memorandum                                    FPPC

To    : Diane Fishburn                        MAR 16 10 48 AM '82 Date  : March 12, 1982
       Fair Political Practices
       Commission                                                File No.:


From  : ROGER D. WOLFERTZ
        Legal Office, RM:535 (5-4694)

Subject: Council For Private Postsecondary Educational Institutions


In accordance with our recent telephone conversations concerning
the question whether council members are subject to filing finan-
cial disclosure statements under the Political Reform Act, I am
attaching a copy of the protocol developed and used by the prior
council.  At their first meeting the Department of Education will
recommend that the new council established pursuant to Chapter
1099 Statutes of 1981 (SB 272) adopt this protocol as an interim
operating instrument.

As you know, my opinion is that members of the council are not
subject to the financial disclosure law for those reasons pre-
viously discussed.  Taking a cue from you, one form of resolution
is for the council to include, in any formal internal, operating
manual or other formal written procedures, an adaptation of the
wording in Government Code Section 97100.  I suggest the following:

"No member of the council shall make, participate in,
making or in any way attempt to use his or her offi-
cial position on the council to influence a decision
of the council in which he or she knows or has reason
to know he or she has a direct financial interest."

I would appreciate receiving your advice in letter form no later
than March 29, 1982, as this is an agenda item for the new coun-
cil's first meeting on March 30th.

I appreciate all the attention you have been giving this problem.


[signature]
ROGER D. WOLFERTZ
Assistant Chief Counsel

RDW:pb
attach.
cc:  Xavier Del Buono
     John Peterson

California State Department of Education

COUNCIL FOR

PRIVATE POSTSECONDARY EDUCATIONAL

INSTITUTIONS


P R O T O C O L




Adopted: January 1977

Last Revised: May 1st, 1989

ARTICLE I. COUNCIL FOR PRIVATE POSTSECONDARY
EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTIONS

100. Status

The Council for Private Postsecondary Educational Institutions exists
under the authority of Education Code, Section 94304.

102. Membership

The membership of the Council is comprised of 15 voting members, 14
of which are appointed by the Superintendent of Public Instruction
subject to the concurrence of the State Board of Education, and one
Superintendent of Public Instruction or his designee, and three ex
officio nonvoting members (Education Code, Section 9.3 04(a)).

103. Tenure

(a) All voting members, with the exception of the Superintendent of
Public Instruction or his designee, who are appointed after January
15, 1974, serve for a period of four years, and no appointee shall
serve for more than eight consecutive years.

(b) Appointed members who fail to attend two consecutive meetings
and who do not have valid reasons for such absences forfeit the
office, thereby creating a vacancy (Education Code, Section 94304(b)).

ARTICLE II. OFFICERS

106. Chairperson

At each regular organizational meeting, the members of the Council
shall elect a Chairperson from the membership of the Council who shall
serve for one year or until his successor is elected. No officer shall
serve more than two terms. He shall preside over the meetings and per-
form such other functions as are necessary and proper for the conduct
of the Council's business. He shall also serve as the Council's desig-
nated member of the California Postsecondary Education Commission, or
shall appoint a designee thereto.

107. Vice Chairperson

At each regular organizational meeting, the members of the Council
shall elect a Vice Chairperson from the membership of the Council who
shall serve until his successor is elected. It shall be the respon-
sibility of the Vice Chairperson to perform duties of the Chairperson
in the absence or temporary incapacity of the Chairperson.

108. Duration of Office; Vice Chairperson as Chairperson

The Vice Chairperson shall perform those duties described in Section 107
only until the Council can elect a Chairperson to fill the unexpired

term of a Chairperson who for any reason is permanently unable to
carry out the duties of that office.

109. Executive Secretary

The designee of the Superintendent of Public Instruction shall serve
as Executive Secretary of the Council.

It is the duty of the Executive Secretary to process communications
to the Council, to keep the minutes of the proceedings of the Council,
to furnish each member with a copy of the minutes, and to carry out
other duties as directed by the Council. He shall promptly prepare
and submit regular recommendations of the Council to the Superintendent
of Public Instruction and submit the Superintendent's replies to the
Council.

110. Nominations

(a) At the regular meeting immediately preceding each organizational
meeting, the Chairperson of the Council shall appoint a Nominating
Committee whose task it is to nominate one Council member for each
election to the offices of Chairperson and Vice Chairperson.
Members of the Nominating Committee may themselves be nominees for
office.

(b) The Nominating Committee shall obtain each nominee's acceptance
of the nomination.

(c) After the Nominating Committee has presented its report, and
before voting for the different offices takes place, the Chairperson of the Council shall call for further nominations from
the floor.

(d) Should a committee nominee withdraw before the election, the
Nominating Committee shall meet immediately to agree upon another
nomination. Otherwise, the committee is discharged upon completion of the elections.

ARTICLE III. MEETINGS OF THE COUNCIL

113. Organizational Meeting and Regular Meetings

Beginning in June 1977, an organizational meeting of the Council
shall be held during the month of June each year, but the Council
may fix another date which may be one month sooner or one month later.
The Council shall fix the time and place of the meetings other than
the organizational meeting. Insofar as practicable, meetings shall
be held alternately in the northern and southern sections of the state.
Meetings shall not be held except on written notice of reasonable
length at least seven days in advance, provided that in an emergency situation,
members may waive notice. Such a meeting may be held with the consent
of a quorum of the Council membership. The Council shall meet no less
than six times each calendar year.

2

114. Order of Business

The order of business at all meetings of the Council, except as otherwise provided, shall be as follows:

(a) Roll call

(b) Approval of the minutes

(c) Communications

(d) Recommendations for action
    Chairperson
    Executive
    Members

(e) Public requests to be heard—nonagenda items

(f) Reports
    Executive
    Chairperson
    Committee
    Members

(g) Other business

(h) Adjournment

The order of business may be changed at any meeting by majority consent of members present, except an election of officers required under Article II, Sections 106 and 107, shall immediately follow the roll call.

115. Agenda

The agenda shall be determined by the Chairperson in consultation with the Executive Secretary. Any member who wishes to place an item on the agenda shall submit a written request to the Chairperson or the Executive Secretary within ten days of the next scheduled meeting of the Council. A member may place an action item on the agenda by unanimous consent of the voting members present.

116. Quorum

A majority of the voting members of the Council shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of business. Action of the Council shall be decided by majority vote of all voting members.

117. Public Hearings

Upon declaration of a public hearing, or at the invitation of the Council, persons desiring to address the Council shall notify the Executive Secretary in writing at least ten days prior to the meeting.

3

The following procedures will prevail:

(a) Speakers shall confine their remarks to the subject indicated in
    their written applications or be declared out of order.

(b) Speakers shall not be allowed to relinquish their time to others
    without good reason and the approval of the Chairperson.

(c) It is desirable that the speaker provide the Executive Secretary
    with a copy of his statement and, if possible, 20 copies for mem-
    bers of the Council.

(d) No person shall speak for more than five minutes unless the time
    limit is waived by request of a Council member present and voting,
    with the concurrence of the Chairperson.

(e) Any group appearing before the Council shall select a spokesman
    who shall address the Council on their behalf. Other members
    of the group may do so only with the permission of the Chairperson.

(f) Speakers shall not be permitted to directly question individual
    members of the Council or the Executive Secretary.

(g) Council members desiring to question speakers must do so through
    the Chairperson.

(h) Copies of these procedures shall be available to interested groups
    and individuals and shall be given to those individuals and or-
    ganizations filing to appear before the Council.

118. Address by Non Council Members

    The Chairperson may recognize persons other than the Council members
    at meetings. Each person shall limit his address to five minutes
    unless the time limit is waived by a majority of the Council members
    present and voting. All remarks shall be germane to the business of
    the Council under consideration and shall be addressed to the Chair-
    person and not to any member thereof. No person, other than the members
    of the Council and the person having the floor, shall be permitted to
    enter into any discussion.

ARTICLE IV: PROCEDURES

122. Parliamentary Procedure

    In matters of parliamentary procedure not covered in this Protocol,
    the provisions of Roberts Rules of Order shall prevail.

123. Amendments

    This Protocol may be amended at any regular meeting of the Council
    by a majority of the voting members of the Council, provided a writ-

notice of such amendment, including the proposed text of the amendment, has been submitted at the previous regular meeting.

ARTICLE V: EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE

125. Membership

There shall be an Executive Committee consisting of the Chairperson, Vice Chairperson, and Executive Secretary and two other Council members chosen to ensure that there shall be no fewer than two public members and two industry members on the Executive Committee.

126. Meetings

The Chairperson may call meetings of the Executive Committee upon consent of a quorum of the Committee.

127. Duties and Powers

The Executive Committee shall follow established Council policy in conducting the interim business of the Council, subject to review and ratification at the first meeting of the Council after action is taken by the Executive Committee. The Executive Committee shall not rescind any action of the Council.

128. Quorum of the Executive Committee

Any three members of the Executive Committee shall constitute a quorum.

129. Reports to the Council

The Chairperson shall report in writing to the Council at its next regularly scheduled meeting any actions of the Executive Committee.

ARTICLE VI: STANDING COMMITTEES

132. Establishment

Standing committees may be established by the Council and shall include an Approvals, Complaints, and Appeals Committee.
[?] REVIEW

ARTICLE VII: AD HOC COMMITTEES

134. Establishment

Ad hoc committees may be created to examine specific issues and/or perform specific tasks. Such committees shall be appointed by the Chairperson of the Council. Ad hoc committees shall meet at the call of the Chairperson of the Ad hoc committee, shall report to the Council,

5

and shall terminate when action has been taken by the Council. They
shall be governed by the rules of the Council.

ARTICLE VIII: COUNCIL POLICY STATEMENTS

136. Council Policy

Other than the Chairperson, no member, officer, or committee chairperson
shall represent the Council in making any public statement, oral or
written, without the authorization of the Council except when previous
Council policy has been established and is in effect. Any statement
made by the Chairperson not specifically authorized by the Council
shall be later reviewed by the Council. This section does not affect
the expression of personal opinions.

ARTICLE IX: APPEAL PROCESS

138. Initial Resolution Negotiations by OPFE Staff

Staff of the Office of Private Postsecondary Education (OPFE) is responsible for working with each institution on issues of noncompliance
with appropriate standards in the operation of private postsecondary
institutions. When staff becomes convinced that an institution is
unable or unwilling to be in compliance, the institution will be notified in writing of the area of concern, including specific applicable
Education Code provisions, regulations or other standards, and the
failure to correct the matter will jeopardize its authorization or
approval to operate. The institution will have an opportunity to
respond and make necessary corrections within a reasonable time.

139. Prehearing Procedures

(a) If the institution is unwilling or unable to comply with the
required standards, the Council, upon OPFE's request will hold
a hearing on the question whether an action to deny, suspend,
or revoke the approval or authorization, as the case may be,
should be initiated.

(b) OPFE staff will prepare a case file for the Council, with the
following considerations:

(1) Judgments must be made as to what material is to be disclosed to the Council. Since disclosing information to
the Council is making that information public, a decision
must be made as to whether more harm would result from making
the information public than from withholding the information.

(2) The information to be provided in the case file is that
which is directly related to each item of violation or
deficiency based on OPFE investigation. A list of such
items is to be cited, referred to the Education Code,

Title 5 regulations and any other locally applicable standards. Backup information and procedures used by OPEE
staff to verify any deficiency or violation shall be included.
Accusations made by complainant are not to be included
unless verified by OPEE staff.

(c) At a Council meeting held prior to the meeting in which a hearing
may be scheduled, the case file will be presented to the Approvals,
Complaints, and Appeals Committee only, at which time the committee
will advise OPEE whether the case file contains sufficient information with which to proceed to a hearing. The committee's advice,
along with any contrary OPEE position, will be reported to the
Council as information.

(d) Representatives of the institution will be invited to appear
personally at the hearing for the purpose of presenting the institution's position on the issues. The invitation to appear
will be sent to the institution at least 65 days prior to the
scheduled hearing and will include a copy of the case file.

(e) Any documentation that the institution wishes to present to the
Council for purposes of the hearing must be received by OPEE
at least 15 days prior to the hearing. The Council, by majority
vote of the voting members present, may receive new or additional
documentation at the hearing from OPEE or the institution.

(f) Both the case file and any documentation received from the institution will be mailed to each Council member prior to the
meeting at which the hearing is to be held.

(g) The Approvals, Complaints, and Appeals Committee will meet at
a time prior to the hearing that will allow sufficient opportunity for discussion of the issues and procedures.

140. Standard for Review

The Council's consideration of the evidence and argument presented
at the hearing is a part of the charging process, as distinguished
from the guilt finding process. Therefore, the evidence of deficiencies or violations, as presented to the Council by OPEE, must
be deemed to be true.

The standard for review to be used by the Council is whether reasonable
cause exists to deny, suspend, or revoke the approval or authorization.
To conclude that reasonable cause does exist, the Council must find that:

(a) The evidence presented is sufficient to support a finding of
fact that a violation or deficiency has occurred, and

(b) The violation or deficiency is, after considering any evidence
of mitigation or rehabilitation and any other requirement in
Section 1883(c) of Title 5, California Administrative Code,
sufficient to support a denial, non-renewal or revocation of
the approval or authorization to operate.

7

141. Conduct of the Hearing

It is intended that advisory hearings under this Article be conducted
informally, in that the legal rules of procedure and evidence will not
apply. However, the following rules and procedures will apply:

(a) A representative of OPPE will present the case to the Council,
following which the institution may present its position in oral
or written form, or both. Although the institution may call
witnesses and be represented by legal counsel or any other person
or persons, the hearing is neither an adversary proceeding, nor one
where allegations of fact must be proven. OPPE staff, along with the
Department of Education's legal counsel, will act as technical resource to the Council.

(b) Evidence and argument presented by the institution shall be
material and relevant to the deficiencies or violations raised
by OPPE and that is within the scope of the standard for review
described in Section 149.

(c) Irrespective of the number of persons speaking in behalf of the
institution, the institution shall have an aggregate total time
of one hour in which to present its position. An extension of time
may be granted by the Council upon a majority vote of the voting
members present.

(d) Following the hearing and any deliberation, the Council shall
advise OPPE whether, in the opinion of the Council, there is
reasonable cause to deny, suspend or revoke the institution's
approval or authorization, as the case may be. Should the institution fail or refuse to participate in the hearing, the
Council shall base its advice solely on the information provided
by OPPE.

142. Post Hearing Procedures

(a) OPPE will consider the Council's advice and will decide whether
to take formal action to deny, suspend, or revoke the approval
or authorization.

(b) If formal action is taken to deny, suspend, or revoke, the institution may request an administrative hearing pursuant to the
Administrative Procedure Act. Until the Superintendent of Public
Instruction makes his final decision on the hearing officer's
proposed decision, action is still considered pending and the
institution may continue to operate but a proposed new institution
may not start operating.

ARTICLE X: COMPLAINT PROCESS

145. Council Policy

The Council shall, in accordance with the procedures described in Section
146 of this Article, review complaints from educational institutions,
agents and consumers, and make recommendations to the Superintendent
regarding their disposition. Council review of complaints against
OFPE is limited to complaints regarding OFPE policy and administration,
by OFPE, of policy and law. In no event shall review be made of com-
plaints relating to the professional conduct, competency, or personal
character of the Department of Education staff.

146. Procedures

The following procedures apply in reviewing complaints:

(a) All complaints, including those received by the Council initially,
are to be filed with the Chief of OFPE.

(b) OFPE will contact the complainant in order to acknowledge receipt
of the complaint and to make a determination regarding any question
of OFPE's jurisdiction in the matter.

(c) If, after investigation, OFPE is unable to resolve the matter,
the complaint will be referred to the appropriate associate
Superintendent of Public Instruction for further effort toward
resolution.

(d) If the Associate Superintendent is unable to resolve the matter,
the complaint, and all details of the investigation, shall be
referred to the Council through its Approvals, Complaints, and
Appeals Committee. The Council will review the matter, including
the committee's advice, and recommend to OFPE a course of action
for resolution.

(e) OFPE will report to the Council the disposition of all complaints
referred to the Council for review and recommendation.

(f) OFPE may withhold from Council review any complaint against any
institution or agent which is already under investigation for
possible denial, revocation, or suspension of an approval or
authorization or an agent's permit. Any intended adverse action
with respect to an approval or authorization to operate will be
referred to the Council under Article IX (Appeals Process).

(g) OFPE will establish and maintain a digest of complaint resolutions.
